name: Siqi [Edward] Guo
about:
Incoming **Robotics Ph.D. student (ME) at Georgia Tech** (Fall 2026) and CMU M.S. ECE graduate. Currently a Humanoid Robotics Intern at Yondu AI (Y Combinator), developing parallelized C++ motion planning and inverse kinematics pipelines for wheeled humanoid warehouse robots.

Building real robotic systems has made one limitation consistently apparent to me: classical pipelines achieve reliability through structure, but that structure breaks down in unstructured environments where the system encounters situations outside its designed operating conditions. I believe the root cause is that current robots lack learned representations capable of reasoning about the downstream consequences of actions across perception, dynamics, and task semantics. Learned approaches, whether through generative world models, Vision-Language-Action (VLA) architectures, or reinforcement learning, are the direction I find most compelling for closing this gap. Yet deploying such approaches on physical robots surfaces a second, inseparable challenge: the scenarios where generalization matters most are precisely those that are rarest in any collected dataset. This makes data efficiency and long-tail coverage not a separate research interest, but a practical prerequisite for building systems that are both intelligent and robust beyond the lab.

At **Cepton** (LiDAR systems), I designed embedded control algorithms for precision mirror actuation and rebuilt the firmware scheduling architecture, gaining a close view of where control guarantees break down under physical variability.

At the **CMU Biorobotics Lab** under Professor Howie Choset and Research Scientist Lu Li, I contributed to **EigenBot**, a bio-inspired modular hexapod, focusing on locomotion algorithm design, firmware development, and performance evaluation (ICRA 2025). Under Dr. Matthew Travers, I worked on **MMPUG**, a heterogeneous multi-agent autonomous system, implementing the global planner and leading real-world deployment on RC cars.

Before CMU, I researched adversarial robustness and unsupervised graph representation learning under Prof. Di Jin and Ph.D. student Bingdao Feng at Tianjin University, publishing at **AAAI 2023** and in the **IEEE Journal of Neural Networks**. I also completed a full-time software development internship at E2Biz SoftTech during my undergraduate studies.
